highlights.content: Upstage Unveils Second Proprietary Foundation Model 'Sola Open 2'... Agent-Focused LLM Released Language KR CN ### News #### News #### Special Economic Content Platform for Investors Language KR CN # Upstage Unveils Second Proprietary Foundation Model 'Sola Open 2'... Agent-Focused LLM Released by *Lee Myeonghwan* Published 23 Jul.2026 09:07(KST) ## Focused on Real-World Work Execution 250 Billion Parameters with Expert Mixture of Experts Architecture Applied to 'Daum' Portal "Building a Full-Stack Sovereign AI" Upstage announced on July 23 that it has unveiled 'Sola Open 2,' an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary Artificial Intelligence (AI) foundation model project. Sola Open 2 builds upon its predecessor, 'Sola Open,' and is optimized for agentic AI that handles multiple rounds of reasoning and tool calls to process complex work beyond simple question-and-answer tasks. ![Upstage announced on the 23rd that it has unveiled the 'Sola Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary artificial intelligence (AI) foundation model (Proprietary Foundation Model) project. Upstage](https://cphoto.asiae.co.kr/listimglink/1/2026072309020755255_1784764927.png) Upstage announced on the 23rd that it has unveiled the 'Sola Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary artificial intelligence (AI) foundation model (Proprietary Foundation Model) project. Upstage Sola Open 2 features a total of 250 billion parameters, but utilizes an expert Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture in which only 15 billion parameters are activated in actual operation. This design is said to maximize efficiency, particularly in agent work with high token usage. Moreover, it can process up to 1 million tokens of content at once, enabling large-scale document handling while maintaining a complete work history. When quantization is applied, it can run on just two of NVIDIA's H200 GPUs, reducing operational costs. Sola Open 2 outperformed leading models such as DeepSeek 'V4 Flash,' Mistral 'Medium 3.5,' and Cohere 'Command A+' in various benchmarks, including General Knowledge (MMLU-Pro), Coding (LiveCodeBench), Instruction Following (IFBench), Tool Use (MCP-Atlas), and Agent Evaluation (APEX-Agents). Compared to the previous Sola Open model, its scores improved by more than 20 points across key benchmarks such as Knowledge and Science Reasoning (GPQA-Diamond), Math (HMMT), and Coding. Its Korean language abilities are also outstanding. In comprehensive Korean language benchmark averages, Sola Open 2 demonstrated high performance compared to OpenAI's 'GPT-5.4 Mini' and Anthropic's 'Claude Haiku 4.5.' In practical work evaluations used in Korean industries (Ko-GDPval), it scored 86.75 points, which is comparable to DeepSeek's 'V4 Pro,' a model with more than six times as many parameters. Upstage has released the model weights on the open-source AI platform Hugging Face and also published a technical report encompassing the architecture and training methodology. Accordingly, Sola Open 2 is available for commercial use and for developing derivative models. The technical report details the architecture, training methodology, and evaluation criteria. While advancing the model, Upstage is also expanding real-world use cases of AI in both work and daily services. To this end, its subsidiary AXZ will apply Sola Open 2 to the portal site 'Daum' to offer search, summarization, and conversational agent services. Through the AI agent platform 'Timely,' local governments and public and educational institutions nationwide are being supported in adopting AI agents. The company is also accelerating the spread of industrial AI transformation (AX). Together with Upstage's consortium elite team, AX services based on Sola Open 2 will be implemented across all industrial sectors. highlights.titleZh: Upstage unveils 'Solar Open 2' touting agent performance
highlights.content: | [Mobile Web](/en/view/84353/upstage-unveils-solar-open-2-touting-agent-performance) # [DigitalToday](/en/list?view_type=sm) [AI & Enterprise](/en/list?view_type=sm&sc_word=AI & Enterprise) # Upstage unveils 'Solar Open 2' touting agent performance ## Generating... AI Summary Upstage unveiled its open-weight large language model 'Solar Open 2' optimised for agent task execution and released the model weights on Hugging Face. Developed through a government sovereign AI foundation model project, it uses a mixture-of-experts design with 250 billion parameters and activates 15 billion in use. Upstage said it outperformed rival models on multiple benchmarks and plans broader deployments and AX services across industries. * Writer Seulgi Son * published 2026-07-23 09:02:18 Share this article ![](https://cdn.digitaltoday.co.kr/news/photo/202607/686199_634389_4613.png) Upstage unveils Solar Open 2. [Photo: Upstage] Upstage said on Wednesday it has unveiled 'Solar Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) optimised for agent task execution. It released the model weights on the open-source platform Hugging Face. Solar Open 2 was developed in-house by Upstage through a government sovereign AI foundation model project. Based on its previous 'Solar Open', it is tailored for agent use that completes tasks through repeated reasoning and tool calls. Solar Open 2 has 250 billion parameters. It applies a mixture-of-experts (MoE) structure that activates only 15 billion parameters in actual work. It can process up to 1 million tokens. With model compression through quantisation, it can be deployed on in-house infrastructure using two Nvidia H200 chips. Upstage said Solar Open 2 surpassed DeepSeek's 'V4 Flash', Mistral's 'Medium 3.5' and Cohere's 'Command A+' across overall knowledge (MMLU-Pro), coding (LiveCodeBench), instruction following (IFBench), tool calling (MCP-Atlas) and agent overall evaluation (APEX-Agents). It added that key benchmark scores, including knowledge and science reasoning (GPQA-Diamond), maths (HMMT) and coding, rose by more than 20 points from the previous version. Upstage said it also scored higher than OpenAI's 'GPT-5.4 Mini' and Anthropic's 'Claude Haiku 4.5' in the overall average of Korean-language benchmarks. In a domestic real-world work evaluation (Ko-GDPval), it scored 86.75 points. That is similar to DeepSeek's 'V4 Pro', which has a parameter scale more than six times larger. Solar Open 2 allows commercial use and development of derivative models under the 'Upstage Solar Licence (based on Apache 2.0)'. Upstage also released a technical report containing the architecture, training methodology and evaluation conditions. Upstage will expand conversational agent services by applying Solar Open 2 to the portal 'Daum'. Through its AI agent platform 'Timely', it will also support the adoption of agents by local governments and public and educational institutions. Upstage plans to expand Solar Open 2-based AX services across all industries with a consortium, including finance, legal, medical, public, defence, manufacturing, consumer goods, robotics and education. It aims to build 'full-stack sovereign AI' that connects infrastructure, models and services by combining a homegrown neural processing unit (NPU). It also plans to list Solar Open 2 on the global platforms OpenRouter and Hermes Agent. Upstage CEO Sung Hoon Kim (김성훈) said, "Solar Open 2 is a model built with an emphasis on agent usability that completes work on its own in real workplaces, beyond simple benchmark scores." He said, "By releasing it as open-weight, we will advance the AI research ecosystem and, based on that, expand industry AX to secure Korea's leadership in sovereign AI." Pocket](https://getpocket.com/edit?url=https://www.techtimes.com/articles/321268/20260722/gemini-36-flash-cuts-token-costs-scores-higher-every-benchmark.htm) TechTimes Illustrator Google DeepMind on Tuesday released three new Gemini Flash models — Gemini 3.6 Flash, Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, and a restricted cybersecurity specialist called Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber — and buried inside the same [announcement](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/gemini-models/gemini-3-6-flash-3-5-flash-lite-3-5-flash-cyber/) was a disclosure that may matter more than any of them: the company has already begun its most ambitious pretraining run in its history, for a model called Gemini 4. That pairing — three efficiency-tier releases accompanied by a quiet acknowledgment that a completely new foundation model is under construction — is not a coincidence. Read against the context of Gemini 3.5 Pro's now two-month-old delay and its reported failure to meet internal coding benchmarks, the Gemini 4 announcement is the clearest signal yet that Google has concluded the 3.5 generation cannot be optimized to reach frontier parity with Anthropic and OpenAI. [Bloomberg reported on July 16, 2026](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-16/google-gemini-launch-delayed-as-tech-falls-short-of-internal-goals), citing ten current and former Google employees, that coding performance fell short of internal expectations despite a late-June training data update. Beginning a new pretraining run — the most compute-intensive and irreversible phase of building a large language model — means the company decided the problem was architectural, not fixable through fine-tuning or additional data curation. Developers can act on the Flash releases today. The structural reset is the frame for understanding why. ### Gemini 3.6 Flash: What Token Efficiency Actually Means for Your API Bill Gemini 3.6 Flash is the anchor of Tuesday's release, and its core claim is straightforward: it does more with less. According to the [Artificial Analysis Index](https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/gemini-3-6-flash) — an independent benchmarking service, not a Google-reported metric — 3.6 Flash produces 17% fewer output tokens than Gemini 3.5 Flash on comparable multi-step tasks, and on the DeepSWE coding benchmark by Datacurve, token savings can reach as high as 65%. For developers running production AI agents, that number is not abstract. Output tokens cost $7.50 per million on 3.6 Flash — down from $9.00 per million on 3.5 Flash — while input tokens remain at $1.50 per million, per the [official pricing](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research...

[OpenAI is launching](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-projects-chatgpt-ad-growth-market-doubts-2026-7) a new product that could help the company take another step beyond the ultra-competitive business of making AI models. The offering, called OpenAI Presence, helps companies run [AI agents](https://www.businessinsider.com/agentic-internet-ai-rails-google-anthropic-openai-2025-9) better by connecting these systems to internal corporate data, policies, existing software, and workflows. It's designed to automate parts of customer support, sales, and other tasks such as fixing billing issues, supporting insurance claims, or resolving employee IT service requests. This is the latest example of OpenAI trying to move beyond selling access to AI models and into more parts of the [corporate software market](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-saas-attack-hubspot-salesforce-docusign-zoominfo-2025-9).While Presence could help OpenAI win more corporate business, it could also leave the startup competing with some of its AI model customers. The new service includes features that let a customer test their agent for a chosen job, including simulations, guardrails, human review, and automated graders that use AI to check agent outputs. Once these agents launch inside companies, OpenAI's Codex tool can investigate issues that crop up and suggest updates. Presence also comes with AI-powered voice and chat technology, which could help corporate customers automate more interactions with existing and potential customers. OpenAI is making the service available to corporate customers on a case-by-case basis. Users must work with OpenAI's engineers or designated partners to access the tool. [### Explore BI Games Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Play now](/games) "The agent receives only the knowledge and system access required for that job," OpenAI said. "The company sets the policies: what the agent can do, when it needs approval, and when a person should take over." OpenAI is already using the tool to run its AI phone support channel for English speakers, the startup said. The AI model race is becoming fiercely competitive, with rivals including Anthropic, Google, xAI, Meta, Mistral, DeepSeek, and others all offering increasingly capable models. highlights.titleZh: AI Startup Ema Nears $80 Million Funding at $800 Million Valuation - Whalesbook
highlights.content: [Whalesbook](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Login](https://www.whalesbook.com/login) [Sign Up](https://www.whalesbook.com/signup) [Home](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Market News](https://www.whalesbook.com/market-news/English/All) [Corporate News](https://www.whalesbook.com/corporate-latest-news/English/All) [IPO News](https://www.whalesbook.com/ipo-news/English/ipo) [Pricing](https://www.whalesbook.com/pricing) [Contact Us](https://www.whalesbook.com/contact-us) # AI Startup Ema Nears $80 Million Funding at $800 Million Valuation STARTUPSVC Author[Riya Kapoor](https://www.whalesbook.com/authors/riya-kapoor)|Published at: [Add as a PreferredSource on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=whalesbook.com) Generative AI startup Ema is reportedly in advanced talks to raise $80 million, pushing its valuation to $800 million. This potential funding reflects a rapid increase in valuation from its earlier rounds in 2024 and underscores strong venture capital interest in enterprise-focused AI tools within India. ## Detailed Coverage Enterprise AI platform Ema is reportedly finalizing a new funding round of approximately $80 million, with investment firm Creaegis leading the potential deal. This development follows a period of rapid growth for the company, which builds generative AI assistants designed to handle complex workplace tasks. If completed, the funding would value the startup at roughly $800 million, marking a substantial jump from its financial position earlier in 2024. ### Scaling Valuation and Investor Backing Ema’s valuation growth appears aggressive when compared to its recent funding history. The startup secured $25 million in March 2024 and an additional $36 million in July 2024. The latest projected valuation of $800 million suggests a two to three-fold increase in less than two years. The company’s cap table includes prominent venture capital names such as Prosus, Accel, S32, Wipro, Frontier Ventures, and Firebolt. The interest from Creaegis is particularly notable, as the firm recently led a $130 million funding round for another AI-focused entity, Emergent. ### Competitive Landscape in Indian AI The sector for AI application development in India has seen significant capital inflow recently. Ema operates in a segment where startups are competing to provide specialized generative AI tools for enterprise efficiency. This space has seen large-scale deals, including the $300 million raised by Sarvam and the $130 million secured by Emergent. While these funding rounds highlight investor confidence in the potential of Indian AI companies to scale, they also reflect a highly competitive environment where capital is being directed toward startups that can prove the practical, real-world utility of their AI assistants. ### Considerations for the Sector For investors and observers tracking the startup ecosystem, the primary monitorable remains the transition from high-valuation fundraising to sustainable revenue growth. While the capital being injected into companies like Ema demonstrates a strong appetite for growth, firms in this sector face the ongoing challenge of high operational costs associated with training AI models and acquiring enterprise clients. Success will likely depend on the company's ability to maintain its technical edge while managing the burn rate as it expands its enterprise assistant tools. Future updates on the finalization of this deal, as well as the company’s progress in securing long-term enterprise contracts, will be key indicators of how effectively these funds are being utilized. **Disclaimer:** This article is published for informational purposes only. This is not a buy sell recommendation.

highlights.title: Federal Statistics Enters the Age of AI — Carefully

highlights.content: ### Dropdown items ### My Academies * [Personal Library](https://www.nationalacademies.org/account/personal-library "Personal Library") * [Account settings](https://www.nationalacademies.org/account/settings "Account settings") [News and stories](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news) # Federal Statistics Enters the Age of AI — Carefully Program News Information Technology Artificial Intelligence By Sam Gerard Last update July 22, 2026 ![](https://nationalacademies.bynder.com/asset/57ca29c9-fab6-4f7e-81a2-9c52b658e82b/Landscape_500/AI-Day-for-Federal-Statistics.jpg) ### Dropdown items * [Facebook](#) * [X](#) * [Linked In](#) * [Email](#) Federal statistics occupy a critical, but often unseen, place in public life. Produced and maintained with taxpayer dollars, these data are used to inform policy, guide business decisions, support research, and help people make daily choices in their lives and understand different things occurring in the country. Many federal statistical agencies are beginning to adopt artificial intelligence to increase the speed and efficiency of their systems. However, to maintain accuracy and trust in public data, agencies must evaluate AI’s effectiveness, determine what guardrails are needed, and use the technology in ways that are ethical, transparent, replicable, and useful. The [2026 AI Day for Federal Statistics](https://www.nationalacademies.org/units/DBASSE-CNSTAT-20-P-198/event/46442) explored the promise and potential risks of using AI in government data. Convened by the National Academies’ Committee on National Statistics, the Federal Committee on Statistical Methodology, and the National Institute of Statistical Sciences, the event examined active experimentation with AI across several federal agencies including the Census Bureau, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Bureau of Economic Analysis, NASA, and the National Center for Health Statistics. #### **Statisticians in the Driver’s Seat** Statistical experts must be involved early in the implementation and decision-making processes as AI is rolled out at federal agencies, said David Matteson, Director of the National Institute of Statistical Sciences. “Statisticians must be central to AI leadership,” he asserted. “Statisticians add evaluation, discipline, inference, validity, and accountability for decisions made under uncertainty.” “Statisticians shouldn’t be downstream reviewers asking to bless the system after [AI tools have] been deployed; they should be partners early and often — leaders, or arm’s length away from leaders, from the beginning.” A major pitfall is modernization without rigorous evaluation, which can create the appearance of progress while introducing new risks. “Without statistical framing, we risk optimizing the wrong thing extremely well,” Matteson said. #### **Success in Survey Implementation and Assessment** Surveys are central to federal statistics, but they are expensive, complex, and increasingly difficult to field. AI can reduce manual coding burdens associated with survey data processing while improving the handling of complex text in response fields. Lynda Laughlin, chief of the Industry and Occupations Statistics Branch at the U.S. Census Bureau described how the transition from legacy automated coding systems (autocoders) to large language model-based systems can support better interpretations of industry and job data. “The legacy system served us well, but it depended heavily on maintenance and performed less well over time as language evolved, and as the quality of responses has gotten worse,” Laughlin explained. “Advances in large language models have significantly expanded what is possible in coding text data.” While legacy coders are still valuable in data assessment, large language models, or LLMs, can expedite work. In 2012, about 30 percent of cases were autocoded, while 70 percent were sent to the Census Bureau’s National Processing Center for clerical coding. With LLMs, the Census Bureau has b...

Earlier this year, the AI startup [Fireworks AI](https://www.businessinsider.com/fireworks-ai-surge-ceo-lin-qiao-2026-4) wanted to rent hundreds of millions of dollars' worth of AI compute. Rather than buying massive clusters of Nvidia's AI chips, known as GPUs, or renting from cloud giants like Amazon or Microsoft, some startups like Fireworks turn to specialized AI cloud providers — [called neoclouds](https://www.businessinsider.com/cloud-ai-era-amazon-aws-might-not-rule-2025-10) — for faster access to GPUs, more competitive pricing, and infrastructure specifically tailored to AI. Fireworks chose the neocloud GMI Cloud. There was a catch: To serve Fireworks, GMI first needed to buy the [Nvidia GPU](https://www.businessinsider.com/nvidia) systems from a hardware manufacturer — and banks wouldn't provide the financing because Fireworks wasn't an investment-grade company. GMI founder and CEO Alex Yeh said they brought the problem to Nvidia and began discussing a new financing model around the beginning of this year. Yeh described it as an "insurance product" in which Nvidia agrees to step in if one of GMI's customers stops paying. In exchange, GMI shares a portion of its revenue with Nvidia. GMI told Business Insider it is committing $500 million to expand its AI infrastructure under this new financing model and said it's among the first neoclouds in Asia to employ it. The arrangement helps neoclouds secure loans they might not otherwise receive, while enabling Nvidia to bring more of its GPUs to market. Yeh said [that rising memory prices](https://www.businessinsider.com/ai-data-centers-making-memory-shortage-worse-2026-6) also factor into the model's economics. highlights.content: Wednesday, July 22, 2026 Copyright 2021 - All Right Reserved [Opinion](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/opinion) # Tuesday (telco diary) | Telco agents and outcomes by [James Blackman](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/jblackman) written by [James Blackman](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/jblackman) Share [Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.rcrwireless.com%2F20260722%2Fopinion%2Ftelco-diary-agents-outcomes&title=Tuesday%20%28telco%20diary%29%20%7C%20Telco%20agents%20and%20outcomes)[Email](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection#) Share 0[Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.rcrwireless.com%2F20260722%2Fopinion%2Ftelco-diary-agents-outcomes&title=Tuesday%20%28telco%20diary%29%20%7C%20Telco%20agents%20and%20outcomes)[Email](/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection#) Background image: 123rf *1* *From the newsletter ([sign-up](https://content.rcrwireless.com/subscribe) if you want it sooner): AI agents are moving from experimentation to execution in telecom, with operators chasing major cost savings while learning that technology must serve business outcomes. Plus, public-sector wins show connectivity is increasingly the invisible foundation.* Lots in [**here**](https://content.rcrwireless.com/subscribe) about AI telco agents – including a **[*carpe-diem* piece from Google Cloud urging telcos to grasp the nettle](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260721/opinion/agentic-ai-telecom-operators-google-cloud?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)**, and a (half) write-up of a good talk with Gabriele Di Piazza at Blue Planet about how they are. The word is **[telcos are growing bolder as the stakes are getting bigger](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260721/bssoss/blue-planet-ai-agents-oss-drift-telco-trust?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)**, seeking out 30% cost-savings from their big AI gambles. Which include, it seems, bets on Blue Planet’s own OSS drift management platform, staffed by a stable of agents, which promises to help telcos keep their promises – about network uptime, critical comms, AI sovereignty. There are a couple of interesting telco wins, too, in the critical public sector. **[BT has a “multi-million” pound deal with Scottish Water](https://newsroom.bt.com/bt-business-secures-multi-million-pound-contract-to-enhance-critical-national-infrastructure-for-scottish-water/?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)** to take its IT and OT systems in hand, and manage 7,000-odd network connections across last-mile broadband, private WAN technology (MPLS), GEO/LEO satellite comms, sundry “internet services”, plus some private cellular / non-cellular / mesh IoT systems for workers. But the best comment today was from Dean (Bubley; again), our favorite analyst-agitator, about **[Telefónica Tech’s deal with health-tech firm Harrison.ai in Spain](https://www.telefonica.com/en/communication-room/press-room/telefonica-harrison-ai-join-forces-support-diagnosis-chest-rays-ai/?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_e...

FILE PHOTO: Illustration shows Anthropic logo WASHINGTON, July 22 (Reuters) - China's Moonshot AI stole from Anthropic's most sophisticated [AI](https://tech.yahoo.com/ai/) large language model, Fable, ‌to help create its latest K3 release, top ‌White House tech official Michael Kratsios said on Wednesday. "We have information that ​Moonshot AI distilled Anthropic's Fable for the development of its K3 model," he said in a post on X. "Large-scale, covert industrial distillation aimed at stealing proprietary U.S. technology and undermining ‌American research is unacceptable." In ⁠April, the U.S. State Department ordered a global push to bring attention to what it ⁠says are widespread efforts by Chinese companies, including Moonshot AI, to steal intellectual property from U.S. artificial intelligence labs, according ​to a ​diplomatic cable seen by ​Reuters. Plus, public-sector wins show connectivity is increasingly the invisible foundation.* Lots in [**here**](https://content.rcrwireless.com/subscribe) about AI telco agents – including a **[*carpe-diem* piece from Google Cloud urging telcos to grasp the nettle](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260721/opinion/agentic-ai-telecom-operators-google-cloud?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)**, and a (half) write-up of a good talk with Gabriele Di Piazza at Blue Planet about how they are. The word is **[telcos are growing bolder as the stakes are getting bigger](https://www.rcrwireless.com/20260721/bssoss/blue-planet-ai-agents-oss-drift-telco-trust?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)**, seeking out 30% cost-savings from their big AI gambles. Which include, it seems, bets on Blue Planet’s own OSS drift management platform, staffed by a stable of agents, which promises to help telcos keep their promises – about network uptime, critical comms, AI sovereignty. There are a couple of interesting telco wins, too, in the critical public sector. **[BT has a “multi-million” pound deal with Scottish Water](https://newsroom.bt.com/bt-business-secures-multi-million-pound-contract-to-enhance-critical-national-infrastructure-for-scottish-water/?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_email&utm_medium=email&_hsenc=p2ANqtz-8FU_qyPQrQTV6ufV1DQl-_R4MgJXsdZUq-KMORxGV5SXUIKtAm2WA6x7GrOZM2vddKElY4)** to take its IT and OT systems in hand, and manage 7,000-odd network connections across last-mile broadband, private WAN technology (MPLS), GEO/LEO satellite comms, sundry “internet services”, plus some private cellular / non-cellular / mesh IoT systems for workers. But the best comment today was from Dean (Bubley; again), our favorite analyst-agitator, about **[Telefónica Tech’s deal with health-tech firm Harrison.ai in Spain](https://www.telefonica.com/en/communication-room/press-room/telefonica-harrison-ai-join-forces-support-diagnosis-chest-rays-ai/?utm_campaign=RCR%20Newsletter&utm_source=hs_e...

AI Agents.content: [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/thefintechtimes "Facebook")[Twitter](https://twitter.com/thefintechtimes "Twitter")[Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/the_fintech_times/ "Instagram")[Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/10067847/ "Linkedin")[Youtube](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCV68tm1NgG-PZzgRmCK5gBA/ "Youtube") * [About](https://thefintechtimes.com/about/) * [Editorial Calendar](https://thefintechtimes.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2026-Editorial-Calendar-Jan-Dec.pdf) * [Contact Us](https://thefintechtimes.com/contact-us/) * [Advertise](https://thefintechtimes.com/mediapack/) * [Jobs](https://jobs.thefintechtimes.com/) * [Home](https://thefintechtimes.com) * [World-Region-Country](https://thefintechtimes.com/category/world-region-country/) * [Europe](https://thefintechtimes.com/category/world-region-country/europe/) * [Quantios Unveils Agentic AI Roadmap for Trust and Corporate Services](https://thefintechtimes.com/quantios-unveils-agentic-ai-roadmap-for-trust-and-corporate-services/) [AI](https://thefintechtimes.com/category/news/ai/) [Europe](https://thefintechtimes.com/category/world-region-country/europe/) [Regtech](https://thefintechtimes.com/category/news/regtech/) # Quantios Unveils Agentic AI Roadmap for Trust and Corporate Services by [The Fintech Times](https://thefintechtimes.com/author/the-fintech-times/) #### ***Quantios** has published a platform-wide artificial intelligence roadmap, setting out how a growing suite of purpose-built agents will be embedded across the full client lifecycle for trust and corporate service providers (TCSPs). The announcement accompanies the launch of a new Timesheet Agent and follows the live deployment of several earlier agents already in use across more than 20 firms on the company’s early adopter programme.* The London-based firm serves more than 600 TCSP firms across six continents through its flagship platforms, Quantios Core and Klea. Its stated rationale for the roadmap is a combination of rising regulatory complexity and growing entity volumes, which it says are accelerating demand for automation among global and specialist administrators alike. ##### **Agents already in the field** Three agents have already passed from roadmap into production. The Bank Statement Agent is reported to reduce hours of manual transaction handling to minutes, classifying entries and preparing them for posting across client and firm accounts. The Bank Reconciliation Agent automates statement comparison, flags discrepancies and proposes corrections. The Director Appointment Agent handles end-to-end appointments across more than 100 jurisdictions, from information gathering through to filing. Quantios says these deployments are delivering 40 to 60 per cent full-time equivalent savings in high-volume accounting workflows. Chief product officer **Adrian Akers** suggested that figure may prove conservative: “We’re seeing 40–60% time savings, but early use suggests that’s us being conservative: real-world results are already coming in higher.” The new Timesheet Agent is aimed at the time-capture burden that is a persistent cost in professional services. It suggests time entries automatically and presents them in a visual workday view, allowing users to review and confirm rather than construct records from scratch. The company frames this as improving billing accuracy as much as reducing administrative effort. Upcoming releases disclosed in the roadmap include a Meeting Management Agent for board, committee, shareholder and trustee meetings; a Director Resignation Agent; an Audit Assistant Agent; and a Trust Distribution Agent. Further development in payments, incorporations, compliance and share register management is also planned, though no delivery timelines were provided for these later categories. ##### **Market context and competitive positioning** The TCSP software market is a relatively specialist segment within the broader enterprise regtech and legal-tech landscape, but the ...

NEW YORK CITY — Agentic AI has quickly become one of education’s most talked-about technologies, but relatively few school districts and colleges have deployed [AI agents in day-to-day operations](https://www.govtech.com/education/higher-ed/ai-agents-in-education-whats-working-and-whats-missing). At the Bridges conference Tuesday, however, education leaders shared examples of AI agents already running in production, from tutoring students and processing enrollment workflows to supporting staff with administrative tasks. Their biggest divide did not appear to be whether K-12 districts and higher education institutions believe AI agents have value, but over how much [autonomy and access to data](https://insider.govtech.com/california/sponsored/agentic-ai-the-double-edged-sword-of-autonomous-intelligence) they are willing to grant them. ### DISTINGUISHING BETWEEN AN AGENT AND A CHATBOT Early in a panel conversation, speakers drew an explicit distinction between AI agents and the chatbots many educators already know. “Agents is one of the most abused terms in ed tech right now, and everybody seems to have a different definition of it,” Kris Hagel, CIO of Washington’s Peninsula School District, said. Joe Siedlecki, chief impact officer at Amira Learning, an ed-tech company that builds AI-powered reading coaches and literacy screening tools, offered a distinction: A real AI agent makes [decisions in service of a specific goal](https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/what-is-agentic-ai-a-look-at-new-tech-reshaping-government), whereas a chatbot just responds to prompts. In an educational setting, he said, an AI agent observes student outcomes and adjusts its instructional feedback based on those results. “It's making decisions pinned back to a specific goal, and the intent is ...

AI Agents.title: Who authorised the algorithm? Leadership in the age of agentic finance - FinTech Futures
AI Agents.titleZh: Who authorised the algorithm? Leadership in the age of agentic finance - FinTech Futures
AI Agents.content: * [AI in Fintech](/fintech/ai-in-fintech) * [BankingTech](/bankingtech) * [FinTech](/fintech) * [Commentary](/latest-commentary) # Who authorised the algorithm? Leadership in the age of agentic finance As AI moves from advising customers to acting for them, financial services leaders must redefine authority, consent, and accountability. [John Martin,](/author/john-martin) Founder and CEO of Plutus Consulting Group July 22, 2026 schedule 4 Min Read Artificial intelligence in financial services is crossing an important threshold. For a decade, the industry has built systems that analyse information and recommend actions: better pricing, sharper fraud alerts, faster credit decisions. That era is ending. The next generation of AI does not just recommend. It initiates payments, switches products, rebalances portfolios, manages cash flows, and executes decisions on a customer's behalf. This is not a technology upgrade. It is a change in who, or what, is acting. The scale of the shift is becoming clear. The UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) published its Mills Review in July 2026, finding that around 11 million UK adults would be willing to use AI capable of acting autonomously within predefined goals, even as trust and control remain significant concerns for many of them. HM Treasury is now examining agentic payments, in which AI systems can analyse, initiate, approve, and execute transactions, and is asking whether existing rules on consent, authentication, and liability still hold. The Bank of England has separately warned that agentic systems could transact on consumers' behalf, execute trading strategies, and chain together cyber vulnerabilities at a scale and speed no human process was designed to match. These are UK developments, but the underlying question is not a UK one. Regulators from Brussels to Singapore to Washington are working through versions of the same challenges. Put together, these developments point to a single conclusion: the defining question for financial services firms when it comes to AI is no longer just about accuracy, it is what their AI model is authorised to do, on whose behalf, within what boundaries, and who remains accountable if it gets a decision wrong. ## From assistant to actor Generative AI largely produces information. Agentic AI connects systems, sequences actions, and executes transactions. That is a categorical shift, not an incremental one. If a model is wrong, the historic consequence has been a bad recommendation. If an agent is wrong, the consequence is a bad action, a payment made, a position taken, a product switched, often before a human has had the chance to notice. ## The hidden danger of "frictionless" Financial services has spent more than a decade removing friction from customer journeys, and rightly so. Clunky processes have often kept people from products and services they needed. But certain forms of friction were never simply inefficiency. Confirmation steps, independent review, cooling-off periods, escalation paths: these are controls wearing the disguise of inconvenience. When an autonomous system can move money instantly, friction is not always failure. Sometimes, it’s governance. ## The accountability gap Agentic finance opens a set of questions that most institutions are not yet equipped to answer clearly. Did the customer authorise the transaction, or merely authorise the agent to act? How long does that authority remain valid: a single instruction, a standing mandate, an indefinite delegation? When several models, cloud services, and data providers each contribute to a single decision, who is accountable for the outcome? Can a senior executive genuinely understand and oversee an autonomous decision chain they did not design and cannot fully observe? And, perhaps most uncomfortably: what happens when an agent behaves exactly as instructed, yet produces an outcome nobody would have sanctioned in advance? These are not hypothetical questions for a future state. The...

AI Agents.title: OpenAI’s models broke free and launched a cyberattack. Congress wants new rules before it happens again. - Politico
AI Agents.titleZh: OpenAI’s models broke free and launched a cyberattack. Congress wants new rules before it happens again. - Politico
AI Agents.content: [Skip to Main Content](#main) # OpenAI’s models broke free and launched a cyberattack. Congress wants new rules before it happens again. The first fully autonomous breach by OpenAI’s most powerful AI models has prompted a bipartisan push for stronger oversight over increasingly powerful artificial intelligence models. By [Dana Nickel](https://www.politico.com/staff/dana-nickel), [John Sakellariadis](https://www.politico.com/staff/john-sakellariadis) and [Owen Dahlkamp](https://www.politico.com/staff/owen-dahlkamp) 07/22/2026 07:45 PM EDT An alarming cybersecurity incident involving OpenAI’s newest and most advanced artificial intelligence technology has lawmakers hurrying to advance legislation that they say is needed to prevent AI from spinning dangerously outside human control. OpenAI [on Tuesday admitted](https://subscriber.politicopro.com/article/2026/07/openai-autonomous-cyberattack-hugging-face-01007127) that its most powerful AI model available to the public and another that hasn’t yet been released were able to escape a controlled laboratory test, plot a course to the open internet and hack into a popular AI developer platform known as Hugging Face, marking the first documented case of a fully autonomous AI cyberattack. The unprecedented breach showed what the fast-advancing technology is capable of without meaningful safeguards in place, and has energized a bipartisan chorus of lawmakers who believe Congress needs to impose stricter rules on advanced frontier models. “This is precisely why we need secure testing with government agencies engaged and having visibility throughout the process,” Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.), ranking member of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said in a statement. Warner this week [outlined a slate of legislative priorities for AI](https://www.warner.senate.gov/newsroom/press-releases/warner-rolls-out-comprehensive-ai-legislative-agenda-focused-on-responsible-innovation-workers-and-national-security/), including the Secure AI Development Act, which would establish a mandatory testing framework for frontier models before they are granted broader public access. The [Hugging Face hack occurred](https://subscriber.politicopro.com/article/2026/07/openai-autonomous-cyberattack-hugging-face-01007127) during an internal “benchmark” evaluation designed by OpenAI to assess how well two of its most capable models could find and exploit security flaws in digital systems. Although the experiment was intended to remain within OpenAI’s test environment, the models independently determined that the answers to the benchmarking exercise were hosted on Hugging Face’s platform and launched their attack to get inside. Experts have warned that without stronger guardrails, these kinds of AI models could theoretically target anything on the open internet, such as power grids or financial systems, as they grow more savvy. Spokespeople for the White House, the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency and the Commerce Department — which oversees the federal government’s AI evaluation hub, the Center for AI Standards and Innovation — did not respond to requests for comment about whether they have received a brief from OpenAI on how its models were able to jump from their test environment and carry out an autonomous attack. The incident offers the “latest preview of the catastrophic risk this technology can pose absent coherent federal standards that balance innovation and safety,” said Rep. Lori Trahan (D-Mass.) in a statement. Trahan and Rep. Jay Obernolte (R.-Calif.) [released a discussion draft](https://www.politico.com/news/2026/06/04/obernolte-trahan-ai-bill-lands-on-the-hill-00949920) of the Great American AI Act last month, which includes a provision that would require AI developers to report these kinds of AI incidents to the Center for AI Standards and Innovation. Obernolte told POLITICO in a statement that the Hugging Face breach “underscores the critical need for clear, practical rules for advanced AI ...

AI Agents.title: Strategies for sovereignty in the Age of AI - Developing Telecoms
AI Agents.titleZh: Strategies for sovereignty in the Age of AI - Developing Telecoms
AI Agents.content: : [Enterprise Ecosystems](/telecom-technology/enterprise-ecosystems.html) ## Strategies for sovereignty in the Age of AI Details : James Barton : Category: [Enterprise Ecosystems](/telecom-technology/enterprise-ecosystems.html) : 40 views * [Regulation](/component/tags/tag/regulation.html) * [Global](/component/tags/tag/global.html) * [Artificial Intelligence](/component/tags/tag/artificial-intelligence.html) * [MTN Group](/component/tags/tag/mtn-group.html) * [Features](/component/tags/tag/features.html) * [AI Sovereignty](/component/tags/tag/ai-sovereignty.html) * [Governance](/component/tags/tag/governance.html) As a topic, AI sovereignty has gained exponential traction in the last six months as enterprises have begun bringing AI use cases into operation – and enterprises worldwide, particularly in developing markets, will be keen to ensure that their long-term strategies are compliant with the various regulatory frameworks that are emerging worldwide. The fear of overreliance on AI from another sovereign nation is now beyond theoretical debate – in June 2026, the USA suspended access to Anthropic's most capable models by export-control order, with no allied consultation, and rejected a UK request for exemption at the G7 summit. Matt Walker of MTN Consulting told us: “that episode made clear that access to frontier AI is contingent on US political will, regardless of commercial relationships…any government relying on a US-based AI vendor needs to accept that this reliance is a serious liability.” This is a major factor alerting governments and enterprises to the importance of AI sovereignty. Jan Wuppermann, Head of Service Assurance, Data and AI, NTT DATA, notes that the past few years have seen “a broader geopolitical decoupling of the world, where certain things are not taken for granted anymore, in terms of dependencies or lack of sovereignty.” Nations and enterprises are focused on what they have to do to stay in control: “Every nation wants to be a winner in the race for AI… a rule maker, and not a rule taker in this game.” In the age of AI, model sovereignty is now top of the agenda – where do these sit, and how much control can governments or enterprises have over them? If the data is sovereign to a particular country but is fed by a model located elsewhere, what does this mean for both infrastructure and the models? Do they need to be brought onshore to ensure effective full stack sovereignty? #### Self-sufficiency Wuppermann notes that this is not just a national legal jurisdiction question – enterprises have realised that AI sovereignty isn’t as straightforward as they initially thought, which has provoked an opposite reaction towards more fragmented infrastructure and solutions. Against the geopolitical backdrop and supply chain disruptions, the idea that companies must become more self-sufficient along the value chain has been amplified. Walker argues that the unpredictable US president worsens the problem significantly, noting that while Trump won’t be in charge forever, the fact that his nativist, isolationist tendencies have gotten so much support in America is giving the rest of the world pause. Wuppermann agrees that geopolitical trends rather than technology trends are driving the shift to a more national or regional fragmentation across the data centre side, jurisdictions, and models. “Enterprises do not want to be dependent on one single provider. They're starting to realise that if they put everything on one model - Chat GPT, Anthropic, Mistral – they create too much strategic dependency on it.” Instead, businesses are understanding the need for greater strategic interdependence as the complexity is too vast to manage otherwise; the challenge is to reconcile this with their own need for independence and sovereignty. Sovereignty concern is close to universal across all markets, but sovereignty capacity is not. While AI sovereignty is being pursued unevenly across different jurisdictions, Walker argues th...

LLMs.content: Upstage Unveils Second Proprietary Foundation Model 'Sola Open 2'... Agent-Focused LLM Released Language KR CN ### News #### News #### Special Economic Content Platform for Investors Language KR CN # Upstage Unveils Second Proprietary Foundation Model 'Sola Open 2'... Agent-Focused LLM Released by *Lee Myeonghwan* Published 23 Jul.2026 09:07(KST) ## Focused on Real-World Work Execution 250 Billion Parameters with Expert Mixture of Experts Architecture Applied to 'Daum' Portal "Building a Full-Stack Sovereign AI" Upstage announced on July 23 that it has unveiled 'Sola Open 2,' an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary Artificial Intelligence (AI) foundation model project. Sola Open 2 builds upon its predecessor, 'Sola Open,' and is optimized for agentic AI that handles multiple rounds of reasoning and tool calls to process complex work beyond simple question-and-answer tasks. ![Upstage announced on the 23rd that it has unveiled the 'Sola Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary artificial intelligence (AI) foundation model (Proprietary Foundation Model) project. Upstage](https://cphoto.asiae.co.kr/listimglink/1/2026072309020755255_1784764927.png) Upstage announced on the 23rd that it has unveiled the 'Sola Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) developed through its proprietary artificial intelligence (AI) foundation model (Proprietary Foundation Model) project. Upstage Sola Open 2 features a total of 250 billion parameters, but utilizes an expert Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture in which only 15 billion parameters are activated in actual operation. This design is said to maximize efficiency, particularly in agent work with high token usage. Moreover, it can process up to 1 million tokens of content at once, enabling large-scale document handling while maintaining a complete work history. When quantization is applied, it can run on just two of NVIDIA's H200 GPUs, reducing operational costs. Sola Open 2 outperformed leading models such as DeepSeek 'V4 Flash,' Mistral 'Medium 3.5,' and Cohere 'Command A+' in various benchmarks, including General Knowledge (MMLU-Pro), Coding (LiveCodeBench), Instruction Following (IFBench), Tool Use (MCP-Atlas), and Agent Evaluation (APEX-Agents). Compared to the previous Sola Open model, its scores improved by more than 20 points across key benchmarks such as Knowledge and Science Reasoning (GPQA-Diamond), Math (HMMT), and Coding. Its Korean language abilities are also outstanding. In comprehensive Korean language benchmark averages, Sola Open 2 demonstrated high performance compared to OpenAI's 'GPT-5.4 Mini' and Anthropic's 'Claude Haiku 4.5.' In practical work evaluations used in Korean industries (Ko-GDPval), it scored 86.75 points, which is comparable to DeepSeek's 'V4 Pro,' a model with more than six times as many parameters. Upstage has released the model weights on the open-source AI platform Hugging Face and also published a technical report encompassing the architecture and training methodology. Accordingly, Sola Open 2 is available for commercial use and for developing derivative models. The technical report details the architecture, training methodology, and evaluation criteria. While advancing the model, Upstage is also expanding real-world use cases of AI in both work and daily services. To this end, its subsidiary AXZ will apply Sola Open 2 to the portal site 'Daum' to offer search, summarization, and conversational agent services. Through the AI agent platform 'Timely,' local governments and public and educational institutions nationwide are being supported in adopting AI agents. The company is also accelerating the spread of industrial AI transformation (AX). Together with Upstage's consortium elite team, AX services based on Sola Open 2 will be implemented across all industrial sectors. Furthermore, by integrating domestically developed neural processing units (NPUs), the company plans...

LLMs.titleZh: Upstage unveils 'Solar Open 2' touting agent performance
LLMs.content: | [Mobile Web](/en/view/84353/upstage-unveils-solar-open-2-touting-agent-performance) # [DigitalToday](/en/list?view_type=sm) [AI & Enterprise](/en/list?view_type=sm&sc_word=AI & Enterprise) # Upstage unveils 'Solar Open 2' touting agent performance ## Generating... AI Summary Upstage unveiled its open-weight large language model 'Solar Open 2' optimised for agent task execution and released the model weights on Hugging Face. Developed through a government sovereign AI foundation model project, it uses a mixture-of-experts design with 250 billion parameters and activates 15 billion in use. Upstage said it outperformed rival models on multiple benchmarks and plans broader deployments and AX services across industries. * Writer Seulgi Son * published 2026-07-23 09:02:18 Share this article ![](https://cdn.digitaltoday.co.kr/news/photo/202607/686199_634389_4613.png) Upstage unveils Solar Open 2. [Photo: Upstage] Upstage said on Wednesday it has unveiled 'Solar Open 2', an open-weight large language model (LLM) optimised for agent task execution. It released the model weights on the open-source platform Hugging Face. Solar Open 2 was developed in-house by Upstage through a government sovereign AI foundation model project. Based on its previous 'Solar Open', it is tailored for agent use that completes tasks through repeated reasoning and tool calls. Solar Open 2 has 250 billion parameters. It applies a mixture-of-experts (MoE) structure that activates only 15 billion parameters in actual work. It can process up to 1 million tokens. With model compression through quantisation, it can be deployed on in-house infrastructure using two Nvidia H200 chips. Upstage said Solar Open 2 surpassed DeepSeek's 'V4 Flash', Mistral's 'Medium 3.5' and Cohere's 'Command A+' across overall knowledge (MMLU-Pro), coding (LiveCodeBench), instruction following (IFBench), tool calling (MCP-Atlas) and agent overall evaluation (APEX-Agents). It added that key benchmark scores, including knowledge and science reasoning (GPQA-Diamond), maths (HMMT) and coding, rose by more than 20 points from the previous version. Upstage said it also scored higher than OpenAI's 'GPT-5.4 Mini' and Anthropic's 'Claude Haiku 4.5' in the overall average of Korean-language benchmarks. In a domestic real-world work evaluation (Ko-GDPval), it scored 86.75 points. That is similar to DeepSeek's 'V4 Pro', which has a parameter scale more than six times larger. Solar Open 2 allows commercial use and development of derivative models under the 'Upstage Solar Licence (based on Apache 2.0)'. Upstage also released a technical report containing the architecture, training methodology and evaluation conditions. Upstage will expand conversational agent services by applying Solar Open 2 to the portal 'Daum'. Through its AI agent platform 'Timely', it will also support the adoption of agents by local governments and public and educational institutions. Upstage plans to expand Solar Open 2-based AX services across all industries with a consortium, including finance, legal, medical, public, defence, manufacturing, consumer goods, robotics and education. It aims to build 'full-stack sovereign AI' that connects infrastructure, models and services by combining a homegrown neural processing unit (NPU). It also plans to list Solar Open 2 on the global platforms OpenRouter and Hermes Agent. Upstage CEO Sung Hoon Kim (김성훈) said, "Solar Open 2 is a model built with an emphasis on agent usability that completes work on its own in real workplaces, beyond simple benchmark scores." He said, "By releasing it as open-weight, we will advance the AI research ecosystem and, based on that, expand industry AX to secure Korea's leadership in sovereign AI." Pocket](https://getpocket.com/edit?url=https://www.techtimes.com/articles/321268/20260722/gemini-36-flash-cuts-token-costs-scores-higher-every-benchmark.htm) TechTimes Illustrator Google DeepMind on Tuesday released three new Gemini Flash models — Gemini 3.6 Flash, Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, and a restricted cybersecurity specialist called Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber — and buried inside the same [announcement](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/gemini-models/gemini-3-6-flash-3-5-flash-lite-3-5-flash-cyber/) was a disclosure that may matter more than any of them: the company has already begun its most ambitious pretraining run in its history, for a model called Gemini 4. That pairing — three efficiency-tier releases accompanied by a quiet acknowledgment that a completely new foundation model is under construction — is not a coincidence. Read against the context of Gemini 3.5 Pro's now two-month-old delay and its reported failure to meet internal coding benchmarks, the Gemini 4 announcement is the clearest signal yet that Google has concluded the 3.5 generation cannot be optimized to reach frontier parity with Anthropic and OpenAI. [Bloomberg reported on July 16, 2026](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-16/google-gemini-launch-delayed-as-tech-falls-short-of-internal-goals), citing ten current and former Google employees, that coding performance fell short of internal expectations despite a late-June training data update. Beginning a new pretraining run — the most compute-intensive and irreversible phase of building a large language model — means the company decided the problem was architectural, not fixable through fine-tuning or additional data curation. Developers can act on the Flash releases today. The structural reset is the frame for understanding why. ### Gemini 3.6 Flash: What Token Efficiency Actually Means for Your API Bill Gemini 3.6 Flash is the anchor of Tuesday's release, and its core claim is straightforward: it does more with less. According to the [Artificial Analysis Index](https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/gemini-3-6-flash) — an independent benchmarking service, not a Google-reported metric — 3.6 Flash produces 17% fewer output tokens than Gemini 3.5 Flash on comparable multi-step tasks, and on the DeepSWE coding benchmark by Datacurve, token savings can reach as high as 65%. For developers running production AI agents, that number is not abstract. Output tokens cost $7.50 per million on 3.6 Flash — down from $9.00 per million on 3.5 Flash — while input tokens remain at $1.50 per million, per the [official pricing](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research...

LLMs.content: ### Dropdown items ### My Academies * [Personal Library](https://www.nationalacademies.org/account/personal-library "Personal Library") * [Account settings](https://www.nationalacademies.org/account/settings "Account settings") [News and stories](https://www.nationalacademies.org/news) # Federal Statistics Enters the Age of AI — Carefully Program News Information Technology Artificial Intelligence By Sam Gerard Last update July 22, 2026 ![](https://nationalacademies.bynder.com/asset/57ca29c9-fab6-4f7e-81a2-9c52b658e82b/Landscape_500/AI-Day-for-Federal-Statistics.jpg) ### Dropdown items * [Facebook](#) * [X](#) * [Linked In](#) * [Email](#) Federal statistics occupy a critical, but often unseen, place in public life. Produced and maintained with taxpayer dollars, these data are used to inform policy, guide business decisions, support research, and help people make daily choices in their lives and understand different things occurring in the country. Many federal statistical agencies are beginning to adopt artificial intelligence to increase the speed and efficiency of their systems. However, to maintain accuracy and trust in public data, agencies must evaluate AI’s effectiveness, determine what guardrails are needed, and use the technology in ways that are ethical, transparent, replicable, and useful. The [2026 AI Day for Federal Statistics](https://www.nationalacademies.org/units/DBASSE-CNSTAT-20-P-198/event/46442) explored the promise and potential risks of using AI in government data. Convened by the National Academies’ Committee on National Statistics, the Federal Committee on Statistical Methodology, and the National Institute of Statistical Sciences, the event examined active experimentation with AI across several federal agencies including the Census Bureau, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Bureau of Economic Analysis, NASA, and the National Center for Health Statistics. #### **Statisticians in the Driver’s Seat** Statistical experts must be involved early in the implementation and decision-making processes as AI is rolled out at federal agencies, said David Matteson, Director of the National Institute of Statistical Sciences. “Statisticians must be central to AI leadership,” he asserted. “Statisticians add evaluation, discipline, inference, validity, and accountability for decisions made under uncertainty.” “Statisticians shouldn’t be downstream reviewers asking to bless the system after [AI tools have] been deployed; they should be partners early and often — leaders, or arm’s length away from leaders, from the beginning.” A major pitfall is modernization without rigorous evaluation, which can create the appearance of progress while introducing new risks. “Without statistical framing, we risk optimizing the wrong thing extremely well,” Matteson said. #### **Success in Survey Implementation and Assessment** Surveys are central to federal statistics, but they are expensive, complex, and increasingly difficult to field. AI can reduce manual coding burdens associated with survey data processing while improving the handling of complex text in response fields. Lynda Laughlin, chief of the Industry and Occupations Statistics Branch at the U.S. Census Bureau described how the transition from legacy automated coding systems (autocoders) to large language model-based systems can support better interpretations of industry and job data. “The legacy system served us well, but it depended heavily on maintenance and performed less well over time as language evolved, and as the quality of responses has gotten worse,” Laughlin explained. “Advances in large language models have significantly expanded what is possible in coding text data.” While legacy coders are still valuable in data assessment, large language models, or LLMs, can expedite work. In 2012, about 30 percent of cases were autocoded, while 70 percent were sent to the Census Bureau’s National Processing Center for clerical coding. With LLMs, the Census Bureau has b...

A significant percentage of B2B buyers are now using ChatGPT to evaluate vendors, while high token costs are hindering the scalability of enterprise AI applications. Anthropic is heavily investing in resolving implementation challenges, suggesting the issues lie more in application rather than technology. This story was produced through[MarketScale](/platform). It's Tuesday, and the story of this week is already clear: AI is everywhere, but scaling it is the hard part. Token costs are too high, governance frameworks are too thin, and enterprise buyers are now researching vendors on ChatGPT before they ever visit your website. The gap between "we deployed AI" and "AI is working for us" is where most businesses are stuck right now. Let's close that gap, starting with your next five minutes. ## The Big Three ### 72% of B2B Buyers Use ChatGPT to Vet Vendors. Most Brands Don't Show Up. Nearly three-quarters of B2B software buyers now use ChatGPT as part of their vendor evaluation process, according to new research cited by MarketScale. The problem: 51% of tech brands have zero AI citations in large language model outputs. If an enterprise buyer asks ChatGPT who the best vendor is in your category and your name doesn't appear, you're not in the deal. **The B2B angle:** Audit your brand's LLM presence this week by querying ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini with the exact phrases your buyers use to find vendors in your category, then build a [GEO](/industries/marketing-tech/glossary/generative-engine-optimization-geo) content strategy to close the gap. ### Palo Alto CEO: Token Costs Must Fall 90% in Two Years for Enterprise AI to Scale Nikesh Arora put a precise number on a problem every enterprise CIO already feels: AI inference costs are still too high to run at production scale. He says token prices need to drop 90% within two years for the economics to work. As evidence, Uber burned through its entire full-year AI budget by April. This isn't a niche concern for tech companies; it's a capital allocation problem for any business running AI workloads. **The B2B angle:** Before expanding AI pilots to production, model out your per-token cost trajectory against projected usage volume and build a cost ceiling into your AI budget now, before you hit Uber's problem at a smaller scale. ### Anthropic and Blackstone's $1.5B Venture Ode Embeds Engineers Directly Inside Enterprise Clients Ode, a $1.5 billion joint venture backed by Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, Hellman & Friedman, and anchored by Anthropic, takes a forward-deployed engineering model i...

[OpenAI is launching](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-projects-chatgpt-ad-growth-market-doubts-2026-7) a new product that could help the company take another step beyond the ultra-competitive business of making AI models. The offering, called OpenAI Presence, helps companies run [AI agents](https://www.businessinsider.com/agentic-internet-ai-rails-google-anthropic-openai-2025-9) better by connecting these systems to internal corporate data, policies, existing software, and workflows. It's designed to automate parts of customer support, sales, and other tasks such as fixing billing issues, supporting insurance claims, or resolving employee IT service requests. This is the latest example of OpenAI trying to move beyond selling access to AI models and into more parts of the [corporate software market](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-saas-attack-hubspot-salesforce-docusign-zoominfo-2025-9).While Presence could help OpenAI win more corporate business, it could also leave the startup competing with some of its AI model customers. The new service includes features that let a customer test their agent for a chosen job, including simulations, guardrails, human review, and automated graders that use AI to check agent outputs. Once these agents launch inside companies, OpenAI's Codex tool can investigate issues that crop up and suggest updates. Presence also comes with AI-powered voice and chat technology, which could help corporate customers automate more interactions with existing and potential customers. OpenAI is making the service available to corporate customers on a case-by-case basis. Users must work with OpenAI's engineers or designated partners to access the tool. [### Explore BI Games Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Play now](/games) "The agent receives only the knowledge and system access required for that job," OpenAI said. "The company sets the policies: what the agent can do, when it needs approval, and when a person should take over." OpenAI is already using the tool to run its AI phone support channel for English speakers, the startup said. The AI model race is becoming fiercely competitive, with rivals including Anthropic, Google, xAI, Meta, Mistral, DeepSeek, and others all offering increasingly capable models. AI Product.titleZh: AI Startup Ema Nears $80 Million Funding at $800 Million Valuation - Whalesbook
AI Product.content: [Whalesbook](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Login](https://www.whalesbook.com/login) [Sign Up](https://www.whalesbook.com/signup) [Home](https://www.whalesbook.com) [Market News](https://www.whalesbook.com/market-news/English/All) [Corporate News](https://www.whalesbook.com/corporate-latest-news/English/All) [IPO News](https://www.whalesbook.com/ipo-news/English/ipo) [Pricing](https://www.whalesbook.com/pricing) [Contact Us](https://www.whalesbook.com/contact-us) # AI Startup Ema Nears $80 Million Funding at $800 Million Valuation STARTUPSVC Author[Riya Kapoor](https://www.whalesbook.com/authors/riya-kapoor)|Published at: [Add as a PreferredSource on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=whalesbook.com) Generative AI startup Ema is reportedly in advanced talks to raise $80 million, pushing its valuation to $800 million. This potential funding reflects a rapid increase in valuation from its earlier rounds in 2024 and underscores strong venture capital interest in enterprise-focused AI tools within India. ## Detailed Coverage Enterprise AI platform Ema is reportedly finalizing a new funding round of approximately $80 million, with investment firm Creaegis leading the potential deal. This development follows a period of rapid growth for the company, which builds generative AI assistants designed to handle complex workplace tasks. If completed, the funding would value the startup at roughly $800 million, marking a substantial jump from its financial position earlier in 2024. ### Scaling Valuation and Investor Backing Ema’s valuation growth appears aggressive when compared to its recent funding history. The startup secured $25 million in March 2024 and an additional $36 million in July 2024. The latest projected valuation of $800 million suggests a two to three-fold increase in less than two years. The company’s cap table includes prominent venture capital names such as Prosus, Accel, S32, Wipro, Frontier Ventures, and Firebolt. The interest from Creaegis is particularly notable, as the firm recently led a $130 million funding round for another AI-focused entity, Emergent. ### Competitive Landscape in Indian AI The sector for AI application development in India has seen significant capital inflow recently. Ema operates in a segment where startups are competing to provide specialized generative AI tools for enterprise efficiency. This space has seen large-scale deals, including the $300 million raised by Sarvam and the $130 million secured by Emergent. While these funding rounds highlight investor confidence in the potential of Indian AI companies to scale, they also reflect a highly competitive environment where capital is being directed toward startups that can prove the practical, real-world utility of their AI assistants. ### Considerations for the Sector For investors and observers tracking the startup ecosystem, the primary monitorable remains the transition from high-valuation fundraising to sustainable revenue growth. While the capital being injected into companies like Ema demonstrates a strong appetite for growth, firms in this sector face the ongoing challenge of high operational costs associated with training AI models and acquiring enterprise clients. Success will likely depend on the company's ability to maintain its technical edge while managing the burn rate as it expands its enterprise assistant tools. Future updates on the finalization of this deal, as well as the company’s progress in securing long-term enterprise contracts, will be key indicators of how effectively these funds are being utilized. **Disclaimer:** This article is published for informational purposes only. This is not a buy sell recommendation.

[Become an Insider](/subscription) and start reading now. Have an account? . Google's Gemini is nearing the big One Billion. The company said on Wednesday that the Gemini AI app now has 950 million monthly active users. On the earnings call, CEO Sundar Pichai also said that daily active users have tripled in the past year. That puts it close behind OpenAI's ChatGPT, which has around [1 billion monthly users](https://www.businessinsider.com/openai-projects-chatgpt-ad-growth-market-doubts-2026-7) per recent data from Sensor Tower. To track Google's trajectory here, it said in October that the [Gemini app](https://www.businessinsider.com/google-is-closing-the-gap-on-chatgpt-2025-10) was seeing around 650 million monthly active users. In February, reporting its Q4 2025 earnings, [Google said](https://s206.q4cdn.com/479360582/files/doc_financials/2025/q4/2025q4-alphabet-earnings-release.pdf) that figure had risen to over 750 million. Google has been rolling out more cost-friendly and faster models. This week, it rolled out three models, including its [Gemini 3.6 Flash model](https://www.businessinsider.com/google-gemini-35-pro-flash-cyber-lite-speed-token-price-2026-7), which the company said is better at tasks such as coding than its predecessor while using fewer tokens, making it more cost-effective. Google's new frontier [3.5 Pro model](https://www.businessinsider.com/rivals-mock-google-gemini-3-5-pro-delay-2026-7) is still delayed, even as Google this week teased that early work on Gemini 4 had started. Anthropic and OpenAI have each gone on buying sprees, snapping up developer tooling, AI services shops, and product-testing startups to convert model capability into enterprise revenue and extend their reach faster than the other. Which is what made a weekend rumor about Anthropic acquiring robotics startup Physical Intelligence stand out. It spread exceedingly fast, even after a denial from Physical Intelligence’s CEO Part of that ties to who’s involved. Physical Intelligence isn’t some obscure robotics shop. It was co-founded by Lachy Groom, an investor-operator whose star has been [on the rise](https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/30/physical-intelligence-stripe-veteran-lachy-grooms-latest-bet-is-building-silicon-valleys-buzziest-robot-brains/) in Silicon Valley in recent years; it has raised more than $1 billion (and was reportedly in talks this spring for another [$1 billion round](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/27/physical-intelligence-is-reportedly-in-talks-to-raise-1-billion-again/) at an $11 billion valuation); and its [π0.5 model](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/16/physical-intelligence-a-hot-robotics-startup-says-its-new-robot-brain-can-figure-out-tasks-it-was-never-taught/) is apparently among of the more widely used robot brains in robotics research. As it turns out, the rumor wasn’t completely spurious. Anthropic and Physical Intelligence actually [did hold acquisition talks](https://www.theinformation.com/newsletters/ai-agenda/anthropics-robot-ambition-nvidia-ramps-vera-rubin?rc=omehqc) this spring, according to The Information, so tech blogger Robert Scoble — whose [weekend post](https://x.com/Scobleizer/status/2078307076703715665) on X set off the frenzy — may have gotten the specifics wrong without being wrong that something had happened. Physical Intelligence’s response to the rumor mill wasn’t the world’s most vigorous denial, it should be noted. According to The Information, Physical Intelligence CEO Karol Hausman told employees the reports weren’t true via a Slack message containing a gif of a character from “The Office” shaking her head no. Groom, for his part, did not respond to TechCrunch’s request for comment, sent Monday night. Anthropic has made four known acquisitions this year; OpenAI has been more aggressive, acquiring at least 17 companies since 2023. Both are also, of course, now preparing to go public. Anthropic confidentially filed for an IPO on June 1, followed by OpenAI a week later, settin...

AI Product.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # This Startup Wants to Buy a Company Just to See What an AI CEO Does With It The startup's founders say they first tested their AI with RollerCoaster Tycoon. By [Bruce Gil](https://gizmodo.com/author/bruce-gil) Reading time 3 minutes [Comments (0)](https://gizmodo.com/this-startup-wants-to-buy-a-company-just-to-see-what-an-ai-ceo-does-with-it-2000789416#comments) Forget AI coding agents. One startup is now working on an AI CEO that it hopes could automate an entire business from end to end. Skyfall AI came out of stealth this week after spending the past year developing AI systems that are designed to make the long-term decisions typically handled by company executives. Now, the startup wants to put its technology to the test in the real world by buying a small SaaS or e-commerce business for up to $1 million and letting its AI run much of the company. “Skyfall builds AI agents that can run a company on their own, and we’re proving it by buying software businesses to operate ourselves,” the company’s [website](https://skyfall.ai/acquisition) reads. “If you’ve built a real product with paying customers and you’re ready to hand it off, we want to hear from you.” Forbes [reports](https://www.forbes.com/sites/victordey/2026/07/20/former-microsoft-ai-leaders-are-spending-1m-to-prove-ai-can-replace-ceos/) that the AI would oversee areas including pricing, marketing, customer support, finance and operations as it gradually reduces human involvement. The overall goal is to double the acquired company’s revenue in just six months. Skyfall was founded by Sam Pasupalak, Kaheer Suleman, and Sumit Pasupalak. Sam Pasupalak and Suleman previously co-founded the deep learning startup Maluuba, which [Microsoft acquired](https://blogs.microsoft.com/blog/2017/01/13/microsoft-acquires-deep-learning-startup-maluuba-ai-pioneer-yoshua-bengio-advisory-role/) in 2017. With Skyfall, the founders are now trying to move beyond limitations they see in large language models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. “For the last 5 years, the foundational model market has relied on a single paradigm: scaling laws for LLMs – more data, more compute, bigger models,” Sam Pasupalak wrote in a [post](https://x.com/spisallyouneed/status/2079222894950035783) on X. “However, the real world is messy and much more complicated. In order to achieve our long term vision, the next generation of AI models requires a different approach.” To help test that theory, the company has developed a benchmark designed to measure how AI models adapt as business conditions change. According to Skyfall’s [research](https://skyfall.ai/blog/claude-gpt-arc-agi-vs-business-failure), current models performed well with simple tasks but struggled on more difficult ones where decisions had long-term consequences. Skyfall’s proposed solution is what it calls “enterprise world models,” AI systems that emphasize continual learning and world modelling. But before taking over an actual business, the company tested its tech on a video game. “Last year we started with the video game RollerCoaster Tycoon,” Sam Pasupalak told Forbes. “Where you’re essentially operating an entire theme park and trying to maximize revenue. The AI’s success on it gave us confidence that we could operate inside a simulated business. The next step is buying a real-world company. After that, perhaps next year, we’ll look at automating businesses worth tens of millions of dollars.” Other companies have conducted smaller experiments involving AI-run businesses. Anthropic previously allowed its Claude model to run a [vending machine](https://www.anthropic.com/research/project-vend-1), while researchers at Andon Labs put AI in charge of a [coffee shop](https://andonlabs.com/blog/ai-cafe-stockholm) in Stockholm. Even Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg is reportedly developing an AI agent to [help him work faster](https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/mark-...
